question:
Among U. S. adults from 1999-2000, American Indians/Alaska Natives had the greatest prevalence of smoking ( $p_1=0.40$ ); Chinese-Americans had the lowest prevalence $\left(p_2=0.12ight)$. ${ }^b$ Suppose we take an SRS of 1000 individuals from each of these groups ( $n_1=n_2=1000$ ).
(a) Describe the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$.
(b) What is the probability a sample will show a prevalence difference of 0.26 or less?
(c) How likely is it for a sample to show no difference in prevalences?
answer:
(a) The sampling distribution of $\dot{p}_1-\dot{p}_2$ will be approximately Normal with mean $\mu= p_1-p_2=0.40-0.12=0.28$ with standard deviation $s_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{ rac{p_1 q_1}{n_1}+ rac{p_2 q_2}{n_2}} =\sqrt{ rac{(0.4)(0.6)}{1000}+ rac{(0.12)(0.88)}{1000}}=0.01859$. In other words, $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2 \sim \mathrm{~N}(0.28$, 0.01859)
(b) $\operatorname{Pr}\left(\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2 \leq 0.26ight)=\operatorname{Pr}(Z \leq[(0.26-0.28) / 0.01859])=\operatorname{Pr}(Z \leq-1.08)=$ 0.1400 (from Table B in the Appendix)
(c) $\operatorname{Pr}\left(\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2 \leq 0ight)=\operatorname{Pr}(Z \leq[(0.00-0.28) / 0.01859])=\operatorname{Pr}(Z \leq-15.06)=$ 0.0000

meta info.caption: Prevalence of cigarette use in two ethnic groups.
